CURRENT ATFAIRS.

There has been talk recently about the establishing
of an Irish boys’ paper. Everyone will agree that
we ought to have a boys' paper and that there
is a fair field for it. It must be more than a
couple of years ago that quite o controversy appeared
in the LEADER on the question, but so far we are with-
out an Irish boys’ journal. We are told t»l_mt thg com-
bined circulation of English boys’ papers in Treland is
enormous. This is a serious mabter. We believe one
of the boys’ papers circulating in Treland is called the
“* Union Jack,” and we can imagine the sort of Jingo
blood and thunder that is in that if it does not belie its
name. It is bad enough that Irish history is not ade-
quately taught in the schools, bub if, outside school.
hours, our boys have only English boys’ papers to read it

makes the matter worse. We knoyw of some youngsters

who have had some of Sullivan's ““Story of Ireland”’ read
to them by their mother, and the effect was most pro-
nounced. . The lads were at once stimulated into
romantic patriots, which is a very healthy thing to
happen to lads. When we contemplul~ this effect we
may imagine the effect which TEnghsh boys’ papers
must have on Irish boys. We have never read an
Tnglish boys’ paper, but we assume they are of Eng-
lish Jingo patriotism all compact. What sort of Irish:
boys is such reading calculated to turn out &

We ave aware that many earnest people are anxious
{o promote an Irish boys’ paper. Two things are
wanted—the capital and’ the man to run it. In our
judgment the capital is a minor matter in comparison
with the other requisite—an editor with a vocation for
the work. Provided the proper man was to the fore
the capital necessary would not be very much, and the
paper would probably catch on very quickly: but, on
tho other hand, if even a large capital were subscribect
and the promoters then advertised for an editor we
would not have any great faith in the venture. A mar
must have a distinct gift for conducting a boys’ paper: it
is journalism, but journalism of a very special kind-
Though we have never read English boys’ papers we do
not hesitate to express the opinion that a mere imitation:
of them called by an Irish name would not meet the
case.  You nced someone with original ideas for a
paper intended for Irish boys, and if you had that
person the paper could, we believe, be started in a
short time. There are two classes of boys in Treland,
the boy who would like to be a Sarsfield or Hugh
O’Neill and the lad like Carson when he was a baby
boy, who was taught to believe that England was.
everything fine.  Of course you should make your
¢hoice, and go nap on the choice: boys won’t have half-
and-half arrangements. The contemplated Trish boys”
paper should cater for the lad who would like to be
Sarsfield, and so it should go the whole way in that
direction and give the boys plenty of stories of battles
where the Irish won, stories that would sour the little
tempers of the chaps being brought up little Johnnie
Bulls as Carson was brought up, as he recently told
the public. You cannot bridge the Boyne in a boys’
paper. Other features of our Trish boys’ paper would
be matter for the judgment of the man with a vocation
for’ the worl Is there such a man in Ireland ab the
present time? If there is there 1s a chance for i,
for we have no doubt he would find people ready to
provide the ways and means. Some , shallow people
think that.demand creates supply: it does sometimes,
of course, but as & proposition it is less than half true.
There is a potential demand for an Trish boys’ paper; the
requisite money to start it can, we believe, be lmd,‘ the
thing wanted is the man capable of supplying the jour-
nal. . We would be glad to hear from someone bursting
with ideas for an Irish boys’ paper and filled with a
desire to establish one.

Tditovially the * Trish Times " was struck dumb on
Wednesday and Thursday of last week by the resolu-
tion of the Ulster Unionist Council to « take steps’’ to
prevent the holding of & legal meeting ab W ich a
/member of His Majesty’s Cabinet is to be: one of the
speakers.  ‘‘ Alf Fox ™ evidently got the shivers.
Amongst those present ab the Ulster Unionisb (}oupcll
was an ex-Lord Lieutenant, Londonderry. This is &
nice specimen of the Union and Unionism. “We can-
not hold a mecting in Belfast now unless it pleases th.e'
TUlster Unionist Council!,  They are to & take steps |
to prevent law-abiding peopla’ from doing what they

have a legal‘right to do. WHy are‘not Londonderry
and the rest of them clapped in jail ?

We notice that the Right Hon. T. Sinclair, D.L.;
Right Hon. J. Young, D.L.; Mr. W. H. H. Lyons,
D.L.; several J.P.’s, and ‘several M.P.’s were at this
mesting that determined to prevent people from doing
what they have a legal right to do. Does not the King’s
writ run in Belfast? -

'Ihe so-called ‘‘ Majority ** of Nationalists are, as we
pointed out in a recent issue, ‘a very small minority in
the ‘working ‘entity that counts, the entity .of Great
Britain and Ireland. The so-called ‘¢ Minority ** are
part ‘of ‘the 'big majority. They can raise scares at
their pleasure, whilst the real injustices done to the
Nationalists and- Catholics can only partially, if at all,
be ventilated, as we of the real minority have not the
ear of the British Press; and that Press, even when in
favour of Home Rule, is non-Catholic, so that any
bogey about Catholic intolerance or Papal aggression is
usually swallowed rather eagerly. There was some
row at No. 1 Youghal Rural Council g few days ago,
and theleading Dublin'daily of the real majority, the
** Irish Times,” had a heading, “‘ Nice ‘Argument for
Home Rule,”” and we suppose this thing, such as it is,
has' been' exploited in the English and Scottish divi-
sions' of the majority. In view of the fact that we are
a small minority, and: that the never-ending story of
our persecution by’ the majority does not carry very
far, as‘the ear'of the majority is rather deaf to us,
whilst everything from Danny McCann downwards and
upwards is trumpeted ‘to the majority, the Ulster
Unionist“Council have acted very foolishly.  Truly,
they have 'given the show away.” They have, whilst
the English public were looking on, thrown a lurid light
on the‘tyranny, bigotry, and injustice of Unionism and
Unionists in Treland.

For generations the Irish Catholics have been a small
minority, and a badly-mauled minority at that, of the
working entity of Great Britain and Ireland, but the
utterly-incorrect pictures of us as a majority in an Ire-
land also inhabited by a minority of ‘“loyalists ** and
Protestants—many of the latter * scattered Protes-
tants >—has been held up to the gaze of the Big
Brother. Indeed the picture deceives ourselves, for ib
requires thought to keep those of us who are capable of
seeing into the reality of the situation in mind of the
fact that we are, and have been, a persecuted minority
in the ‘working entity that counts, the entity of Great
Britain and Ireland. 3
All the loaded dice are on.the side of the miscalled
minority, and yet, in a.moment of panic, some of their

grip and passwords.of the Freemasons all over Ireland.

THE PROVOST MARSHAL OF JOURNALISM.
Scene.—An inner Sanctum of Trinity College.

(Enter Antony Traill and a few leading bigots of the
Sourface Press.)

Ant.—This College, founded by the good Queen Bess,
Hath in its chiefest mission sadly failed,
Which was to stamp out Popery among
The mere wild Irish of this savage isle
An{d make them Sourfaced turncoats.
1st Sour.—Alas, it failed
To make them mongrels of that proper type,
Who follow conscience, as the cat doth jump,
And by so doing show great moral grit
And splendid civie virtues.
2nd Sour.—I fear it is the fate
2 Of Trish Papists never to display
That iron strength of character and will
Which marks the conduct of the West Britons
here,
AWho fear to stand alone without the strength
Of Bull to back them up.
Ant.—We mag*make ivy of the Irish yet,

A journalistic reformation might
Do wonders in that way.

“Tis cultured cism of that kind

article, inseparably connected -

THE LEADER. ; 597

with the further question as to whether she is to obtain

complete control of her taxation or not. It is admitted
by everyone that the Irish Parliament will obtain con-

trol over diréet taxation, or at least power to increase
divect taxation, under the coming Bill. The question
then resolves itself into whether it will obtain control
over indirect taxation, namely, Customs and Excise.
Before discussing this question I should like to state at
once that I for one do not think that we ought to make
denial of this control and retention of Irish members
at Westminster a reason for refusing an otherwise good
Bill. Itis quite clear that a certain infinitesimal party,
who rely rather on noise than argument, and who have
nothing to lose by the rejection of the Bill, will attempt
to make this question a casus belli. They are already
doing so in advance. But I think it must be obvious
to every thoughtful Nationalist that Ireland cannot
afford to refuse a fairly good Bill for this reason.
Parnell did not do so. Ireland must take as much as
whe can get, and, if that is not satisfactory, look for
more in the future. Tt is, indeed, certain that an Irish
Parliament with an Executive responsible to it, manag-
ing the affairs entrusted to it with economy, efficiency,
and good sense, and supported eventually by an Ulster
ashamed of its hysterical past, cannot be refused any-
thing it reasonably requests. Once prove Home Rule
to be a sound proposition’and development must neces-
sarily follow, without shoclk to England or injury to the
Empire.

Having clearly established what I think is the
common-sense attitude for us to adopt upon this ques-
tion, let us now consider the reasons for and against
the control of Customs and Excise by an Irish Parlia-
ment. The two great arguments against such control
are—first, that the English people might naturally fear
that an Trish Parliament would raise a tariff against
English manufactured goods, end, second, that the Irish
control of Customs and Excise would deprive moxt-
gagees of Irish land, the Imperial Exchequer, and the
Irish landlords of a necessary guarantee for their
money. The holders of Irish Land Stock are also
interested to the extent that any bother over the collec-
tion of instalments would affect the market price of
the Stock. Let us consider the first objection: that an
Irish Parliament would raise a taviff against Great
Britain.  The very suggestion is absurd. - Grattan’s
Parliament, though frequently provoked by English
tariffs, never attempted to retaliate. The possibility
of such a thing is more remote now. Ireland is a poor
agricultural country, and England, a rich industrial
country, is the natural market for its produce. Would
not an Irish Parliament, elected in the main by an
agricultural population, be insane to dream of raising a
tariff against its best customer? But even admitting
for the moment that such a tariff were probable, what
is simpler than to insert a clause in the Bill\forbidding
either England or Treland to erect tariffs against one
another? This is a simple solution, and one that Ire-
land would unhesitatingly accept. This provision once
inserted in the Constitution could be enforced by the
judges—without even the necessity of intrference by
the Privy Council.© But in this matter I readily con-
fess that were I.an Englishman I should much_ prefer
to leave the question an open one to be dealt with by
the common-sense of an Irish Parliament. 3

English public opinion is, however, ralatively in a
state of absolute ignorance upon this whole question;
although an Englishman, Mr. Erskine Childers has
written by far the most eloquent and logical book* yet
published in support of Ireland’s demand for fiscal and
legislative freedom. This ignorance, due to an entire
absence of discussion on the details of Home Rule since
1893, is by far the greatest-stumbling block to be faced

_ in_attempting to mwive at a favourable and general

solution. The present spivit of the English people is
willing, but their understanding of the question is

*“The Framework of H me Rule,
Childers. (Arnold.) 12s. 6d. e

608

decidedly weak. T is a great pity that Mr. Childer’s
book could not be immediately published in popular
form at a popular price, or a condensation of its argu-
ment in pamphlet form. Having done so much, may
we express the hope that Mr. Childers will place this
crown upon his work and increase our gratitude?

Now the second objection to Irish control of Customs
and Excise, namely, the fact that it would deprive the
Imperial Bxchequer and mortgagees of Irish land of
security for their mouey, seems at first sight an
insuperable one. This is due to a confusion of ideas.
Control of Customs and Iixcise simply means that an

* Irish Parliament will be entitled to say how much these
taxes are to be and on whom they are to fall.
Grant the Irish Parliament this power and it would, to
ny mind, be a matter of secondary importance whether
they were collected by Trish or Imperial officials. Let
them, if necessary, pass through the hands of an
Imperial Receiver-General, with power to recoup the
Imperial Government out of the amounts received by
bhim any loss that might be ined owing to the
default of 1 tenant purc This would be a
most salutary brake upon any insane project, if such
is conceivable outside of a lunatic asylum, to repudiate
Ireland’s national liability for the Land Purchase pay-
ments. Further, let the Imperial Government, the
landlords, aud the. mortgagees ask themselves this
question—Is not a National strike against rent, interest,
and sinking fund instalments on purchase money far
more possible, nay even probable, if Home Rule is
withheld than it could ever be in a free and prosperous
Ireland? Free people-do not repudiate their responsi-
bilities, and in this respect Treland will prove no excep-
tion fo human experience. The arguments in favour
of giving Ireland complete control of her taxation are
overwhelming. First of all, she would escape from the

entanglements of British finance, and could cut her
cloth according to her measure. The question of
representation in the Imperial Parliament would be
immediately sclved by the absolute withdrawal of the
Frish members, but the way would by no means be
blocked to their return in reduced proportions by their
own wish under a sanely conceived federal scheme. Irish
true revenue could be automatically and easily ascer-
tained without the present clumsy and fictitious adjust-
ments. Friction between the Trish and English Parlia-
ments would be practically impossible, the Trish would
be ““ thrown upon themselves,”” which, as John Bright
rightly said, was what a good Home Rule Bill ought to
do.  The propositions of Lord MeDonnell and his
school simply amount to this: that Ireland will con-
tinue to raid the English taxpayer of yearly increasing
sums, and that whilst the Irish Parliament will be
given the power to spend it will be denied the right to
save or to adjust its budget to Ireland’s social needs.

Lord McDonnell, with the best intentions in the world,

is apt to forget that he is dealing with white men.

Ireland is not the Punjab. His scheme has the

supreme merit of being acceptable to no one.

GRAND CANAL COMPANY.

Notice is hereby given that a Half-yearly General
Meeting of the Shareholders will be held at Grand
Canal House, James’s 'Street Harbour, Dublin, on
Thursday, 15th February, 1912, at 8 o’clock p.m., for
the purpose of receiving a Statement of the Accounts of
the Company for the half-year ending 31st December
last, and the Auditors’ Report thereon, together with
the Report of the Directors, to declare a Dividend, to
elect seven Directors, and also to elect an Auditor.

The Books for the transfer of shares will be closed
from the 1st February to-the 15th February, 1912, both
days inclusive.

Copies of the half-yearly account will be distributed
to the Shareholders, and ready for delivery on applica-
tion at the Company’s Offices, James’s Street Harbour.
on and after Thursday, 8th February, 1912.
